narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 40% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students with a higher likelihood of passing.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance in Year 1 and Year 3, represented by your decile rankings, were the most significant factors. These rankings were lower than the average, which contributed to the model's prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam. Additionally, your family's income bracket, which is lower-middle, also played a role, as it might suggest fewer resources available for preparation compared to other students.

Your racial background as Hispanic, while a minority in the dataset, was another factor considered, though it had a less significant impact compared to your academic performance. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA was above average, which worked in your favor, suggesting that you have a strong academic foundation. Similarly, your LSAT score was close to the average, indicating that you have the potential to succeed.

The model also considered your gender and full-time status, but these factors had minimal influence on the prediction. Given this information, you might consider focusing on improving your academic performance in any future studies and seeking additional resources or support to enhance your preparation for the bar exam. By addressing these areas, you can increase your chances of success and potentially reapply with a stronger profile.

Remember, this prediction is not a definitive measure of your abilities or potential. With dedication and the right support, you can work towards achieving your goals.
